text: The surgeon uses a grasper in the left hand to pull the peritoneum around the hernia defect, while a harmonic device separates the peritoneum from surrounding tissues. The defect and its ring are visible, and the peritoneum is being cut to mobilize the ascending colon for mesh placement.

The patient is positioned laterally to facilitate the mobilization of the peritoneum and ascending colon due to gravity. The surgical field is focused on the right lumbar region, where the surgeon cuts down towards the cecum, addressing the defect.

The surgeon completes the peritoneal incision and applies medial and inferior traction to the peritoneum. The sac of the hernia defect is removed, revealing areas of fibrosis from previous open surgery.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection using a disposable platelet from Covidien, carefully mobilizing the ascending colon downward. A pocket is created to accommodate a large mesh, which will be fixed in place using a tacker.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to separate dense adhesions over the hernia defect, applying inferior traction to the peritoneum with the left hand. The fibrotic bands are carefully cut, allowing further mobilization of the hernia sac and the ascending colon. As the peritoneum is separated, the colon drops down, indicating successful mobilization.

The surgeon places a large dual mesh into the paracolic gutter, ensuring it covers at least four centimeters around the hernia defect. The mesh, with a biodegradable intraperitoneal layer and a strong polyester parietal layer, is positioned over the defect and fixed using a tacker. The patient is in the lateral position, facilitating the mesh placement, which will be partially covered by the colon once the patient is moved to a supine position.

The surgeon performs inner crowning by pulling the sac and pushing the mesh into the pocket, fixing it around the Modison's pouch and the capsule of the kidney. Care is taken to avoid firing over the colon, ensuring the lower edge is properly positioned into the pocket. The corner of the mesh is pushed into the peritoneal flap using a grasper, reaching up to the anterior superior iliac spine. The mesh is fixed by pressing and opposing the abdominal wall with the left hand, supporting the tip of the tacker before firing.

The surgeon places additional tackers approximately two centimeters apart for the outer crowning, securing the mesh around the right lobe of the liver and Morison's pouch. The ascending colon is positioned over the mesh, and as the patient is moved from a lateral to a supine position, the colon naturally covers the mesh, eliminating the need for a peritoneal flap. The paracolic gutter and colon are visible, demonstrating the use of an intraperitoneal onlay mesh (IPOM) technique with high-quality mesh.
